standard: CEQ NEPA and Permitting Data and Technology Standard v1.2 (August 18, 2025)
mfr: MFR 4 — Decision-logic export for litigation risk scoring weights
source: NEPA_Agency_Risk_Rate__mdt, NEPA_Circuit_Risk_Weight__mdt, NEPA_Statute_Risk_Weight__mdt, NEPA_Challenge_Prediction_Rule__mdt — calibrated from PermitTEC v0.1 corpus (761 NEPA litigation cases, PNNL 2025) + CourtListener bulk dockets (71M rows, Free Law Project, Stage 14 pipeline)
description: Additive risk weights used by the NEPA_Litigation_Risk_Scorer Flow + BRE Expression Set to compute a per-project litigation risk score (0–100+). Weights are summed across agency, circuit, adjacent statutes, review type, and active challenge prediction rules. Score drives risk tier (Low/Moderate/High/Very High) and triggers the NEPA_Defensibility_Gap_Checker. Version 1.1 adds the v3 composite formula and per-agency litigation duration table from Stage 14 (CourtListener analysis).

scoring_model.formula_v1: risk_score = agency_risk + circuit_risk + sum(statute_risks) + sum(challenge_prediction_deltas) + confidence_modifier
scoring_model.formula_v3: Composite_Litigation_Risk_v3 = (0.2975 * Agency_Loss_Rate) + (0.2125 * Circuit_Loss_Rate) + (0.1700 * Plaintiff_Org_Strength) + (0.0850 * Sector_Volatility) + (0.0850 * Procedural_Posture_Risk) + (0.1500 * Litigation_Duration_Cost)
scoring_model.v3_notes: Duration (Litigation_Duration_Cost) is a COST proxy normalized to [0,1] from observed per-agency median litigation months [BOEM=6.5 min, FTA=33.4 max]. It is not a win-probability signal — median duration is statistically identical for agency wins (15.1 mo) and challenger wins (16.3 mo). V2 component weights scaled uniformly by 0.85 to sum to 1.000.

Conservative default applied when agency not identified

weights.note: Moderate volume; Great Lakes and agriculture

adjacent_statute_risk_weights.description: Additive risk points for each adjacent statute implicated by the project. Multiple statutes stack. Source: PermitTEC challenge-ground frequency analysis.

weights.label: Endangered Species Act
weights.note: ESA Section 7 consultation failures are the top adjacent-statute challenge ground

weights.label: National Forest Management Act
weights.note: Forest plan consistency challenges co-occur with NEPA inadequacy claims

weights.label: Clean Water Act
weights.note: Section 404/401 nexus; dredge-and-fill permits frequently bundled with NEPA

weights.label: National Historic Preservation Act
weights.note: Section 106 tribal consultation; low corpus count but high win rate for tribal plaintiffs

weights.label: Natural Gas Act Section 7
weights.note: FERC certificate cases; NGA-NEPA nexus adds modest incremental risk

challenge_prediction_rules.description: Challenge ground patterns that predict litigation type given review type and adjacent statute. Applied as discrete risk flags that trigger defensibility gap checks.

rules.explanation: EIS processes carry the highest inadequacy challenge rate in the PermitTEC corpus (98.2% of linked litigation). Courts review scope, range of alternatives, and mitigation commitments. Defensibility requires documented alternatives analysis, cumulative effects analysis, and complete administrative record.

EIS processes with long decision timelines (typically 3+ years from NOI to ROD) face supplemental EIS challenges when significant new information or changed circumstances arise post-NOI. NEPA regulations require supplementation when new information materially affects the analysis.

ESA Section 7 consultation is required when a federal action may affect a listed species or critical habitat. Inadequate consultation is the most common adjacent-statute challenge ground. Document biological assessment scope, jeopardy analysis, and reinitiation triggers.

CE determinations for high-impact sectors (energy, mining, large-scale infrastructure) face extraordinary circumstances challenges. Courts look for documented analysis of cumulative effects and any triggered extraordinary circumstances. Ensure the CE determination memo addresses all applicable extraordinary circumstances factors for the project sector and geography.

EA processes that result in a FONSI are vulnerable to challenge that the agency should have prepared a full EIS given the significance of the action. Hard look doctrine and FONSI documentation must demonstrate significance thresholds were properly evaluated.

Tribal Nation challengers achieve 87.5% win rate — highest of any plaintiff category in PermitTEC corpus. Ensure government-to-government consultation is documented, Section 106 review is complete, and tribal sacred site surveys are included in the administrative record.

rules.challenge_ground: GHG Cumulative Impact; Inadequate Alternatives Analysis
rules.explanation: Energy projects in the 4th Circuit have a 28.6% agency win rate — highest-risk sector-circuit cell in PermitTEC corpus. MVP and ACP pipeline decisions inform this rule. Downstream GHG emissions, induced demand, and alternatives range are top challenge vectors.

The #1 NEPA litigation failure mode in the PermitTEC corpus — 42.7% of challenged EIS/EA decisions cite inadequate cumulative or connected actions analysis. When a project has a related federal approval within 10 miles or shares purpose/timeline, connected actions must be scoped together or independent utility documented. Cumulative impacts section (40 CFR 1508.7) required. Citation: 40 CFR 1508.25(a)(1); 40 CFR 1502.16.

Supplementation review required under 40 CFR 1502.9(c)(1) when: ROD issued and new species data received, connected action scope changed, or 5+ years elapsed since ROD. Must attach a written determination by responsible official before tiered decisions or permit reissuance. Failure to supplement is the third most common NEPA litigation failure mode. Citation: Marsh v. ONRC, 490 U.S. 360 (1989); 40 CFR 1502.9(c)(1).

FONSI or ROD cannot be signed until ESA Section 7 consultation status is one of: concurrence received, biological opinion issued, or no-effect determination documented. Project intersects listed species range and consultation remains open. This is a hard blocking condition — the NEPA decision document cannot be finalized while consultation is pending. Citation: 16 USC 1536(a)(2); 50 CFR 402.14; 40 CFR 1502.25(a).

review_type_modifiers.description: Baseline score adjustments by NEPA review type before agency and circuit weights are added

modifiers.note: CE pathway starts with no base modifier; agency and circuit weights determine risk
EA pathway adds 4 points; FONSI challenge is the primary vector
EIS pathway adds 8 points; inadequacy challenge rate is highest of all review types

litigation_duration_by_agency.description: Per-agency median litigation duration in months from CourtListener bulk docket analysis (Stage 14). Used to populate Litigation_Duration_Cost__c. Source: 71M-row docket file, 676/684 PermitTEC cases matched. Duration is INDEPENDENT of outcome (agency-won median=15.1 mo, challenger-won=16.3 mo) — treat as cost proxy only.
litigation_duration_by_agency.source: CourtListener bulk data, Free Law Project, 2026-03-31; Stage 14 pipeline output 14_litigation_duration.json
litigation_duration_by_agency.fallback: agency-only median when sector-cell n<5; global median 15.4 when agency n<4

per_agency.pathway_note: OCSLA direct-circuit review — statutory short window
District court APA; narrow project scope
Section 404 scope issues; summary judgment track
Standardized APA district court track; largest sample
Water cases; intervenors extend briefing schedules
Airport/airspace NEPA; complex noise/air record; low-n
Multi-party urban EIS; extensive intervenors; low-n
